topic: new climbing project — attempting 6c for the first time

turns.content: I have started working on a 6c route at the gym. This is the hardest grade I have attempted and I want to approach it systematically rather than just throwing myself at it and hoping for the best.
Nice — that's a meaningful step up. What's the route like, and where are you getting stuck?

It is a lead route, slightly overhanging in the middle section with a difficult crux about two-thirds of the way up. The crux involves a long reach to a small crimp followed by a heel hook and a dynamic move to a jug. I can do each of those movements individually but I cannot string them together. My success rate on the crux sequence is currently 0 out of 7 attempts.

0 for 7 is frustrating, but it's useful data. If you can do each move individually, the issue is likely one of three things: energy management (you're too pumped by the time you reach the crux), sequencing (the transitions between moves aren't smooth), or mental commitment (the dynamic move is scary when you're already tired). Which one feels most likely?

I think it is a combination of the first two. My forearm pump is definitely higher when I reach the crux compared to when I practice the moves in isolation. And the transition from the crimp to the heel hook requires a body position shift that I am not executing efficiently. I lose about 2 seconds repositioning, which at that level of pump is significant.

That 2-second repositioning window is exactly the kind of thing that makes or breaks a crux at this grade. A couple of suggestions:

For the pump issue — try climbing the route up to just below the crux, then resting on the wall for 30 seconds to simulate having more energy. If you can send the crux after a brief rest, you know the problem is energy management and you need to either climb the lower section more efficiently or build more endurance.

For the transition — practice just the crimp-to-heel-hook sequence on a lower traverse or boulder problem until the body position shift becomes automatic. The goal is to make it feel like one movement, not two.

The rest-and-try approach is a good diagnostic. I will try that next session. For the transition, I think part of the problem is that I am a data person trying to climb like a data person. I want to optimize each move before I connect them, but climbing is actually more fluid than that. The sequence has to become one continuous motion, which requires a kind of physical intuition that I cannot spreadsheet my way into.

That's a really important insight. At the lower grades you can muscle through with technique and planning, but 6c is where climbing starts demanding flow — the ability to trust your body to execute a sequence without consciously managing each step. It's a bit like the difference between reading individual notes and playing music. Have you tried filming yourself on the crux to see what the transition actually looks like from the outside?

I have not, but that is an excellent idea. Visual feedback would give me data I cannot get from how it feels. I will ask someone to film my next 3 attempts and then compare them. If I can identify the exact moment where the transition breaks down, I can target that specific movement pattern. Thank you for suggesting that — it combines my preference for analysis with the physical reality of the problem.

Exactly — video bridges the gap between feel and data. You'll probably notice things you had no idea you were doing, like dropping a hip too early or not looking at the next hold before you move. Three attempts should give you a decent comparison set. What's your timeline for sending this route?

I have given myself 6 weeks, which is approximately 12 climbing sessions. If I am not making measurable progress by session 8, I will reassess whether I need to work on specific weaknesses — finger strength, core tension, endurance — before returning to the project. I do not believe in banging my head against a problem without a feedback loop.

That's a solid plan — a defined timeline with a built-in checkpoint. Session 8 as a reassessment point gives you enough attempts to see a trend without wasting time if something foundational needs work. Good luck with the video analysis next session. I think you will find it revealing.
